cards.title: What a campaign objective actually is
cards.front: Before targeting or creative — what is the very first thing you tell Meta about a campaign?
cards.back: Your objective is simply what you want people to DO when they see your ad. Facebook's algorithm then puts your ad in front of the people most likely to achieve that exact objective. First decide the category, then pick the specific objective.
cards.example: Want webinar sign-ups? Tell Meta 'Conversions' and it finds people like those who already register.

cards.title: Three Categories of Facebook Campaign Objectives
cards.front: Facebook sorts every objective into three buckets mapped to the customer journey. What are they?
cards.back:
Three Categories (Ch. 26):
- AWARENESS — generate interest/awareness (Brand Awareness, Reach)
- CONSIDERATION — get people thinking/seeking info/interacting (Traffic, App Installs, Engagement, Video Views, Lead Generation)
- CONVERSION — drive purchase/use/higher commitment (Conversions, Product Catalog Sales, Store Visits)
Decide the category first, then the specific objective.

cards.title: Campaign > Ad Set > Ad build sequence
cards.front: What is the standard three-level order for building any Facebook ad?
cards.back:
Facebook Ad Creation Order (Ch. 11):
- CAMPAIGN — set the objective; name it descriptively (e.g., 'Like Campaign')
- AD SET — define audience, placements, and budget (how you name ad sets matters more than the campaign name)
- AD — the creative: pick images and write copy

cards.title: You can't change the objective on a live campaign
cards.front: You launched with the wrong objective. Can you just switch it on the running campaign?
cards.back: No. You cannot change the objective on a live campaign — pick the wrong one and you must rebuild from scratch. Decide BEFORE building. Choosing the objective is a pre-launch, not a mid-flight, decision.

cards.title: New campaigns are paused on creation
cards.front: You placed the order and walked away. Why is your campaign spending $0?
cards.back: New campaigns are PAUSED on creation (Ch. 28) — they won't spend until you activate them. After placing the order, remember to activate it (and add more ad sets first if desired).

cards.title: The two AWARENESS objectives
cards.front: When the goal is just to be seen, which two objectives does Facebook offer?
cards.back:
AWARENESS objectives:
- Brand Awareness — shows ads to people more likely to pay attention
- Reach — maximize number of people reached + control frequency
Both can include CTA buttons (but ads won't be optimized for clicks). Formats: single image, single video, carousel, slideshow. Brand Awareness adds Canvas.

cards.title: Reach objective for local/physical businesses
cards.front: How should a local, physical business use the Reach objective?
cards.back: Maximize reach over the campaign, cap frequency, and use location targeting. Use 'Everyone in this location' to target only people traveling nearby. Ideal for local/physical businesses wanting maximum local exposure.

cards.title: The five CONSIDERATION objectives
cards.front: Most marketers start in the middle bucket. Which five objectives live there?
cards.back: CONSIDERATION objectives (Ch. 26): Traffic, App Installs, Engagement, Video Views, Lead Generation. These get people thinking, seeking info, and interacting more than awareness ads. Most marketers start here — though it may or may not be the best choice.

cards.title: Traffic optimizes for clicks, not conversions
cards.front: Why is 'if I just get enough traffic, I'll convert something' a flawed mindset?
cards.back: Traffic optimizes for cheap CLICKS, not leads/sales. You get tons of cheap clicks and no conversions — the ad did exactly what you asked. Don't measure Traffic by conversions. Use Traffic for content amplification (a great blog post) or driving app engagement.

cards.title: App Installs objective
cards.front: How does the App Installs objective differ from Traffic?
cards.back: App Installs is nearly identical to Traffic, but it sends clicks to an app store and optimizes for mobile traffic.

cards.title: Engagement objective's three sub-types
cards.front: The Engagement objective splits into three jobs. What are they?
cards.back:
Engagement sub-types (Ch. 26):
- Boost your posts (post engagement)
- Promote your page (page likes)
- Raise event attendance (event responses)

cards.title: Pre-test content with Page Post Engagement
cards.front: How do you find out if a piece of content resonates BEFORE betting conversion budget on it?
cards.back: Use Page Post Engagement to pre-test: Facebook shows it to people likely to engage, so you can see if content resonates. Then later turn the winning post into a conversion ad. A real case: an arts & craft pre-launch sold out in under 24 hours using PPE pre-launch, then retargeting the engagers.
cards.example: Arts & craft pre-launch: PPE to test, then retarget engagers → sold out in <24 hours.

cards.title: Run multiple audiences against ONE post
cards.front: Boosting from your page timeline only lets you reach one audience. How do you test many audiences against the same post?
cards.back: Use the Page Post Engagement objective in Ads Manager and duplicate the ad set to run many audiences simultaneously — instead of the single audience a timeline boost gives you.
cards.example: $1/day vs. social media marketers, $1/day vs. fans of Mari Smith, plus the boosted post's audience.

cards.title: Event Responses vs. Conversions for events
cards.front: Free festival vs. paid workshop — which objective for each event?
cards.back: Event Responses targets people likely to join a Facebook-hosted event and auto-adds it to their calendar — great for FREE public events, grand openings, festivals. RULE: for PAID events use Conversions instead, not Event Responses.

cards.title: Lead Generation (lead form ads)
cards.front: How do lead form ads kill the landing-page bounce-rate problem entirely?
cards.back: Lead Generation captures contact info via an in-Facebook form that auto-populates — eliminating the landing page and the click-to-opt-in drop-off ('No bounce rate here'). Though technically not categorized under Conversions, treat it as one.

cards.title: The three CONVERSION objectives
cards.front: The 'holy grail' bucket has three objectives. What are they, and what's the holy grail?
cards.back: CONVERSION objectives (Ch. 26): Conversions, Product Catalog Sales, Store Visits. Conversions are the 'holy grail' — put a dollar in, get at least $1.01 back out. These drive purchase/use/higher commitment.

cards.title: Conversions objective requires the pixel
cards.front: What's the non-negotiable prerequisite before running a Conversions objective?
cards.back: The Conversions objective REQUIRES the Facebook pixel installed. It tells Facebook to show your ad to people highly likely to convert and reports cost per conversion (e.g., cost per lead). No pixel, no conversion optimization.

cards.title: The Power of the Pixel
cards.front: Why does Meta's targeting get better after every single conversion?
cards.back: Selecting an objective + a specific conversion event tells Facebook's AI to find more users like your converters. The pixel feeds data back on every fulfilled objective, so targeting improves after every single conversion.
cards.example: Website Conversions optimized for webinar registrations → Facebook shows ads to people like those registering.

cards.title: Conversion event optimization limits
cards.front: How many custom conversions and standard website events can you optimize toward?
cards.back:
Conversions objective optimization options:
- Custom conversions per ad account: up to 40
- Standard website events: 9

cards.title: Four Most-Used Ad Types for Conversions
cards.front: Match the right offer to the right ad type — what are the four most-used conversion ad types, by commitment level?
cards.back:
Four Most-Used Ad Types for Conversions (Ch. 17), reverse order of commitment:
- Short copy ad to a blog post
- Short copy ad to a simple lead magnet
- Long copy ad to a complex lead magnet, webinar, or product
- Video ad to a lead magnet, webinar, or product
The higher the ask, the more pre-framing and longer copy/video needed.

cards.title: Product Catalog Sales + Dynamic Product Ads
cards.front: Selling hundreds of SKUs — how do you avoid configuring an ad for every single product?
cards.back: Product Catalog Sales + Dynamic Product Ads (DPAs) are best for ecommerce / multi-item sellers (e.g., travel). Build ONE ad template that auto-pulls images/details from your catalog and shows the most relevant products based on browsing behavior — no per-ad configuring. Runs on all Facebook platforms.

cards.title: DPAs for retargeting
cards.front: How do you automatically remind a shopper about the exact product they almost bought?
cards.back: DPAs for retargeting (Ch. 15) auto-promote the specific products a visitor viewed. Set rules so anyone who hit add-to-cart/checkout for a product gets reminded before stock runs out. It's 'low-hanging fruit' — get something running, get fancy later.
cards.example: Anyone who added 'Fall Selection Boots' to cart gets reminded before stock runs out.

cards.title: Store Visits setup for multi-location businesses
cards.front: A chain with many locations wants foot traffic. What's the Store Visits setup?
cards.back:
Store Visits combines physical locations + geo-targeting + people with location services on. Setup:
- Add all business locations in Facebook (bulk upload available)
- Target a specific mile radius OR let it update dynamically by desired reach
- Overlay custom/interest/demographic audiences per location
Ads dynamically update per location.

cards.title: Flight Plan Stage 1 — takeoff (0–100 conversions)
cards.front: In the Facebook Flight Plan, what do you run from 0 to 100 conversions?
cards.back:
Stage 1 — 0 to 100 conversions (takeoff). Run only TWO proven campaigns for momentum, traffic, conversions, and DATA. Don't build warm audiences yet:
- 'Like' Ad Campaign — page-like ad, NO external destination; user keeps scrolling Facebook after liking
- Website Conversions Link Post Ad — clickable Link Post Ad to a simple landing page (run under Website Conversions). Alternatives: Boosted Post (optimizes Engagement, not Conversions) or Lead Ad.

cards.title: Flight Plan Stage 2 — climb (101–1,000 conversions)
cards.front: Once you hit ~100 conversions, what third ad type does the Flight Plan layer on?
cards.back:
Stage 2 — 101 to 1,000 conversions (climb). Layer on a THIRD ad type (guideline, not a hard rule — some are ready after only 20–25 conversions). The third type is a video ad, but does NOT have to be video:
- Long Copy Link Post Ad (value/story-based, like a mini blog post), OR
- Content with Call-to-Action (CTA) Video Ad
Which to use depends on situation, assets, style, landing-page content, and how big your 'ask' is.

cards.title: Flight Plan Stage 3 — DPAs and retargeting
cards.front: What does Stage 3 of the Flight Plan introduce at the end of Phase 3?
cards.back: Stage 3 (Ch. 15) introduces video ads, retargeting hot audiences, and Dynamic Product Ads (DPAs) at the end of Phase 3.

cards.title: Boosted posts optimize for Engagement, not Conversions
cards.front: Why won't a boosted post ever truly optimize for sales?
cards.back: Boosted posts won't optimize for Conversions — the algorithm optimizes them for Engagement. That's fine for early momentum/data, but not for true conversion optimization.

cards.title: Lead Ads don't auto-redirect to your thank-you page
cards.front: After someone submits a Lead Ad, do they land on your confirmation page?
cards.back: No — Lead Ads don't auto-redirect to your thank-you/confirmation page. Users stay on Facebook; only ~20–25% click through to your site. Facebook pre-populates contact fields to reduce friction, but plan for the low click-through.

cards.title: Page Like campaigns — allocate 5–10% of budget
cards.front: Why spend 5–10% of budget on Page Likes when likes don't directly sell?
cards.back:
Page Like campaigns — allocate 5–10% of ad budget. Four reasons:
- Twice the newsfeed frequency — up to 4 ads/day to fans vs. 2/day to non-fans
- Social proof — 'so-and-so and 20 others like this page' creates FOMO
- Connection targeting — run ads to friends of fans; 'Advanced Combinations' targets an interest AND friends-of-people-connected to your page
- Highest ROI — fans are a warm market (as long as you didn't buy fake likes)

cards.title: Local branding campaign — small budget, no CTA
cards.front: How should a local business spend a small slice of budget on pure branding?
cards.back: Branding campaign (Ch. 12): allocate a small portion of budget; may have no direct CTA. Builds top-of-mind awareness and 'invisible targeting lists.' Examples: boost a restaurant post ('Come in, mention Facebook, get a free appetizer'); real estate/auto — boost a video or article so it runs all month.
cards.example: Restaurant: photo of people having a great time + 'Come in, mention Facebook, and get a free appetizer.'

cards.title: Video: Conversion objective vs. Video Views
cards.front: A viral, super-engaging video that also converts — should you run it on Conversions or Video Views?
cards.back: If a video's SOLE purpose is conversion, usually start with the Conversion objective. But a super-engaging/viral video that also converts can deliver better ROI/CPA on Video Views, Page Post Engagement, or boosting — Facebook serves a larger, broader audience at much lower CPM/CPC. No hard rule: test. Such a video should have a very high Like-to-Share ratio (~1:1 or better, e.g., 1,248 reactions / 1,276 shares).

cards.title: S3 Sauce — Conversion vs. Video Views head-to-head
cards.front: Same video, copy, audience, budget — how did Conversion and Video Views objectives compare on actual CPA?
cards.back:
S3 Sauce case (same video/copy/audience/budget):
- Conversion objective: 1.58% sales-page CVR, $19.45 CPA, 153,468 video views
- Video Views objective: 0.95% sales-page CVR, $17.68 CPA, 223,458 video views
Video Views won on CPA despite lower CVR — bigger/cheaper reach drove cost per acquisition down. Lesson: test objectives, don't assume Conversion wins.

cards.title: Choosing the right objective — 4 steps
cards.front: Two objectives could both work. How do you decide which to run?
cards.back:
Choosing the right objective (Ch. 26):
- Think through what EXACTLY you want to accomplish
- Choose the objective that fits best (may depend on the quality of your specific ad)
- If two+ could work and you have budget, TEST them
- No budget/time? Go with the objective you have the best feeling about
KEY PRINCIPLE: A great ad + great offer will likely succeed regardless of objective; a sub-par ad/offer will likely fail regardless of objective.

cards.title: Don't default to Conversion for every campaign
cards.front: Why is reflexively choosing 'Conversion' for every campaign a mistake?
cards.back: Don't default to the Conversion objective for every campaign — it's a direct-response reflex. Other objectives often fit better within an overall strategy. Each objective does something different; choose deliberately.

cards.title: Wrong objective = wrong result
cards.front: You see high social shares but low conversions. What's the first thing to check?
cards.back: Wrong objective = wrong result (Troubleshooting). Running Traffic or Page Post Engagement when you want sales gets you traffic/engagement, NOT conversions. When you see high social shares but low conversions, first check that the objective matches the goal — then switch to a conversion objective.

cards.title: Before building any campaign — checklist
cards.front: What must you confirm before you build any campaign?
cards.back:
Before building any campaign:
- Decide which of the three categories (Awareness / Consideration / Conversion) the ad serves
- Drill down to the specific objective within that category
- Confirm the objective matches your ACTUAL goal (don't pick Traffic/Engagement if you want sales)
- Choose the objective BEFORE building — you cannot change it on a live campaign
- If Conversions objective: confirm the pixel is installed
- If a paid event: use Conversions, not Event Responses
- If two+ objectives could work and budget allows: plan a test
- After ordering, remember the campaign is paused — activate it (add more ad sets first if desired)
